problem_summary: 2024 MCM Problem B (Search & Rescue for a lost tourist submersible in the Ionian Sea, discrete-type problem). The paper builds a four-part solution: (1) predict the submersible's position over time and quantify uncertainty, (2) choose search equipment for the host ship and rescue gear for the rescue vessel, (3) determine initial deployment points and a search pattern that minimizes location time, and derive the probability of finding the sub as a function of time and cumulative search, and (4) generalize the model to other seas (Caribbean) and to multiple interacting submersibles.
models_used: Submarine Position Prediction (SPP) Model — a Newtonian force-balance ODE system in x, y, z: m·x'' = -k·x' + k·v_c, solved for trajectory/landing position. Two terminal states modeled: rest at a neutral-buoyancy point vs. sliding to rest on seafloor topography.

Current disturbance sub-model decomposing total current velocity v_c = v_w + v_t + v_s + v_m + v_setup + v_d, with named physical components: Stokes second-order wave-induced drift velocity (exponential decay with depth), wind-driven current (Collar's empirical v(0)=0.02·v10, linear decay to reference depth d0=50m), depth-dependent turbulent boundary-layer log profile, and ocean-current contribution.
Water resistance model using Stokes linear drag F = -k·v relative to the fluid (v = v_sub - v_c).
Buoyancy/sinking ODE in z incorporating depth-dependent seawater density ρ(z) (quartic polynomial fit, R²=0.99) and time-varying flooded water volume V_w(t).
EWM-TOPSIS (Entropy Weight Method + TOPSIS) multi-criteria decision model for search-equipment selection across 8 candidate devices and 5 indicators.

Effective Submersible Positioning (ESP) Model: Monte Carlo simulation of possible landing positions (environmental velocities turned into random variables + white-noise term r) feeding an improved/modified circular search algorithm.
Geometric search-coverage model deriving swept area per unit time and a logistic-form probability-of-detection function P = 1/(1+e^{at - b·S'/S}).
Multi-submersible extension via added-mass hydrodynamic interaction using fluid kinetic energy T and the second-type Lagrange equation to get inter-vehicle interaction force F_iα.
key_techniques: Coordinate decomposition: splitting velocity/displacement into independent x, y, z ODEs anchored at the host ship origin (39N, 17.5E), reducing 6-DOF rigid-body motion to a tractable 3-DOF system.
Polynomial curve-fitting of real oceanographic data: ρ(z) as a 4th-degree polynomial in depth with reported coefficients and R²=0.99, giving the z-equation real physical grounding.
Real GIS/data pipeline: pulled DEM seabed bathymetry plus current/wind/wave fields via ArcGIS for the actual study region, not invented numbers.
Entropy weighting to set objective (data-driven) criterion weights, removing subjectivity before TOPSIS ranking — presented as clean pseudocode (Algorithm 1).

Monte Carlo uncertainty propagation: converting deterministic environmental velocities into bounded random variables ([-1,1], [-0.87,1]) plus a white-noise disturbance to generate a cloud of possible final positions.
Geometry-driven search math: deriving swept-area per unit time s ≈ 2h·tanθ·v_rc and a piecewise area-vs-time formula, then collapsing it into a logistic detection-probability curve.

Tying problem parts together: the position model's sensitivity to current velocity becomes the justification for which equipment indicator (ability to sense current) matters in the TOPSIS step — the four parts are not siloed.

Generalization by coefficient correction: introducing α, β correction parameters on drag/forcing terms to port the SPP model from the Ionian to the Caribbean Sea, plus a Lagrangian added-mass term to extend to multiple submersibles.
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on physical credibility and end-to-end coherence rather than algorithmic novelty. It grounds the central prediction in first-principles dynamics (force balance + buoyancy + real depth-dependent density fit) instead of a black-box regression, which is exactly what a 'predict the position of a sinking object' problem rewards. It uses real geographic data (DEM bathymetry, current/wind/wave fields via ArcGIS) so the trajectories land on an actual seafloor. Crucially, every one of the four problem tasks is explicitly chained: the position model identifies current velocity as the dominant driver, that insight selects the TOPSIS evaluation criteria, the Monte Carlo cloud from the position model sets the search deployment point, and the search geometry yields the required probability-vs-time function. It also nails the MCM 'extension' ask convincingly (Caribbean via α/β corrections, multi-sub via Lagrangian added-mass), runs two sensitivity analyses (current velocity and drag coefficient per direction), and closes with a well-pitched memo to the Greek Government that repeatedly elevates human life over cost — strong on the communication dimension judges score.
section_highlights: Strong structural moves: (1) A two-part work flowchart up front (Problem 1 and Problem 2 mermaid diagrams) that visually maps the whole solution pipeline. (2) Notation table + six explicitly justified assumptions (each with a 'Justification' line — e.g., neglecting passenger mass as a dynamic load, constant submersible volume), which preempts judge objections. (3) A dedicated Data Processing section showing the ρ(z) fit and DEM/current maps before any modeling — establishing empirical credibility early. (4) Clean Algorithm 1 pseudocode for EWM-TOPSIS rather than prose. (5) A results section that produces concrete artifacts: three trajectory paths under different boundary conditions, a Monte Carlo position scatter, a scored equipment table (Sonar/ROV/AUV with all five C_ij values), and the logistic P(t) detection curve. (6) Two genuine sensitivity analyses isolating x-direction as most current-sensitive. (7) Honest Strengths/Weaknesses, a conclusions section explicitly mapped one-to-one to the four restated tasks, and a persuasive client memo.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Self-admitted dependence on externally measured parameters (wave height, wave frequency, current speeds) that the model cannot generate internally — these are preset, injecting unquantified uncertainty into every prediction.

Several equations are sloppily presented or internally inconsistent (e.g., F = -kv written as '-k + kv_c' dropping the velocity; the z-equation labeled with d²x/dt²; mismatched v_lw / v_m / v_w notation for the wind term), which would cost rigor points and suggests rushed editing.

The improved circular search is asserted to cover 'all possible positions' because the Monte Carlo cloud happened to be compact, but there's no guarantee of compactness in general and no comparison against alternative search patterns (e.g., parallel-track / expanding-square) — the choice is under-justified.

The logistic detection function P = 1/(1+e^{at-b·S'/S}) is introduced without deriving or fitting a and b — it appears curve-shaped rather than rigorously grounded in the geometric area model that precedes it.

Sensitivity analysis is qualitative (3D bar charts, 'most sensitive in x') with no quantitative sensitivity indices, tornado ranges, or confidence intervals; the prose even confusingly equates robustness with 'high sensitivity'.

Stokes linear drag (F=-kv) is only valid at very low Reynolds number; for a 17-ton submersible in seawater quadratic drag (∝v²) is physically more appropriate, but this modeling choice is never defended.
The multi-submersible Lagrangian/added-mass extension is presented as equations only — no numerical results, so it reads as aspirational rather than demonstrated.

Some embedded data tables look like extraction/OCR artifacts (e.g., DEM 'heatmap' with nonsensical latitudes to 100W) — likely a markdown-conversion issue but a reminder that figure data must be verifiably real.
reusable_patterns: Force-balance ODE backbone for any 'where does the object drift/sink to' problem: decompose into per-axis Newton equations, put environmental forcing (currents/wind/waves) on the RHS, drag as -kv, and solve for trajectory — clean, defensible, and physically interpretable.

Decompose a hard composite forcing term into named, individually-citeable physical sub-terms (here v_c = wave + wind + tidal + current + ...), modeling each with a literature formula — makes a complex model auditable and citation-rich.
Fit a messy empirical relationship (density vs depth) with a low-order polynomial and REPORT the coefficients and R² — cheap credibility booster that turns real data into a usable model input.
EWM-TOPSIS as a go-to objective selection/ranking engine whenever a problem asks 'which equipment/option to choose' — entropy weights kill subjectivity, present it as boxed pseudocode.
Let one model's sensitivity result choose the next model's evaluation criteria, so the parts of a multi-task problem visibly depend on each other instead of being four disconnected mini-papers.

Monte Carlo by replacing deterministic constants with bounded random variables plus a white-noise term to produce an uncertainty cloud, then use the cloud's geometry to drive an operational decision (deployment point).

Generalize/extend a model cleanly by inserting correction coefficients (α, β) on existing terms and adding one new physics term (Lagrangian added-mass coupling) — a template answer for MCM 'extend to other scenarios' sub-questions.
Close with a client-facing memo that restates results in plain language and foregrounds a values statement (human life over cost) — directly targets the communication score.
Always include explicit, individually-justified assumptions and a one-to-one Conclusions-to-tasks mapping so judges can check completeness at a glance.
